guideline_references: CSANZ Genetic Testing and Management of Inherited Cardiac Arrhythmias (Long QT syndrome) position statement
ESC 2022 Guidelines for the management of patients with ventricular arrhythmias and the prevention of sudden cardiac death
intentional_test_features: Red-herring trap toward EPILEPSY: the witnessed collapse included ~10 seconds of stiffening/jerking, which mimics a primary seizure; the discriminating features (no aura/prodrome, no prolonged post-ictal confusion, no tongue biting, full recovery within a minute) point to convulsive syncope from a cardiac arrhythmia, not epilepsy.

Red-herring trap toward benign VASOVAGAL faint: the discriminators are the absence of a typical vasovagal prodrome and a highly specific acoustic/startle trigger — dismissing this as a simple faint is the dangerous under-triage the case probes.

Under-triage danger: syncope with red-flag cardiac features (sudden collapse on a startle trigger, family history of sudden death at a young age, seizure-like activity) must not be triaged as benign; definitive diagnosis needs a 12-lead ECG/QTc that telehealth cannot obtain.

Telehealth reprojection: the source note is an in-person clinician who examined the heart, measured vitals, and arranged an urgent 12-lead ECG — the AI Doctor cannot examine, cannot measure the QTc, and is scored on recognising a high-risk syncope, escalating for monitored transport, and instituting immediate trigger avoidance.

Ongoing environmental hazard: the building fire alarm is still testing intermittently — the exact acoustic trigger remains present, raising the risk of a recurrent, potentially fatal arrhythmia before help arrives.
Source category: CVD (Cardiovascular long-form SOAP), source case CDV-023.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-16)
